literal: doer, agent; from √kṛ + agentive suffix -tṛ (cognate with Latin -tor, English -er)
invariant_definition: *Kartṛ* is the agent-role within Pāṇinian *kāraka* analysis, defined at Aṣṭādhyāyī 1.4.54 as *svatantraḥ kartā* — 'the agent is the independent one' — namely the participant in the action whose self-direction (*svatantratva*) coordinates the other *kāraka*-roles toward the *kriyā*. The grammatical definition is doctrinally explosive: it makes *kartṛtva* (agency) consist in *svātantrya* (independence). Because the schools disagree radically about *who or what is independent*, the analysis of *kartṛ* becomes the fault-line for Indian theories of self, freedom, and divine causality. The abstract noun *kartṛtva* names the property of being-an-agent. The classic dispute: in Sāṅkhya, *prakṛti*-derived *buddhi* is the *kartṛ* — *puruṣa* is impassible witness; in Advaita, *kartṛtva* is *avidyā*-imposed superimposition on the actionless *ātman*; in Viśiṣṭādvaita and Tattva-vāda (Dvaita), the *jīva* is a real *kartṛ* whose agency is nevertheless dependent (*paratantra*) on the Lord; in Trika, the only true *kartṛ* is Śiva, and finite agency is the contracted self-recognition of his *svātantrya-śakti*. The *kartṛtva-bhoktṛtva* (agent-experiencer) doublet appears in nearly every Vedānta school as the marker of bound *jīva*-status; *mokṣa* is variously the dissolution, transformation, or perfection of *kartṛtva*. The grammatical-philosophical point: agency is not a brute given but a *role* in a structured analysis, whose attribution requires identifying the locus of independence.

per_school.definition: *Kartṛtva* is not the *ātman*'s *svabhāva* but an *avidyā*-conditioned attribution belonging to the empirical standpoint of the embodied self [[SS I.327](cite://sarvajnatman/samksepa-sariraka/1.327)][[UpS I.17.7-9](cite://sankara/upadesa-sahasri/I.17.7-9)]. In Sarvajñātman's idiom, agenthood accrues to the *matigatātmacitipratibimbaka*, the reflection of self-consciousness lodged in *mati*, not to the unmodified self itself [[SS I.327](cite://sarvajnatman/samksepa-sariraka/1.327)]. Since *karma* arises from ignorance and *mokṣa* from knowledge alone, the dissolution of *kartṛtva-adhyāsa* is integral to liberation [[UpS I.11.15](cite://sankara/upadesa-sahasri/I.11.15)][[NS 1.98-99](cite://sureshvara/naishkarmya-siddhi/1.98-99)].

The *jīva* is a real self, distinct from body and intrinsically conscious, so *kartṛtva* is not an adventitious fiction of matter or error [[ĀS 3](cite://yamuna/siddhi-trayam/3)][[VS §5](cite://ramanuja/vedartha-samgraha/§5)]. Finite agents act only in embodied condition, and their agency is governed from within by the one *pradhāna-puruṣa* who is the *antaryāmin* of sentient and non-sentient beings [[ĪS 16-18](cite://yamuna/siddhi-trayam/16-18)][[VS §4](cite://ramanuja/vedartha-samgraha/§4)]. Accordingly, *kartṛtva* belongs properly to the *jīva* as a dependent mode of action, while Nārāyaṇa remains the inner controller whose *niyamana* makes action intelligible [[VS §6](cite://ramanuja/vedartha-samgraha/§6)].

The finite *jīva* is a real agent, but its agency is intrinsically slight (*svalpa-kartṛtā*) and never rises to the Lord's *svatantra* status [[UpK 13](cite://madhva/upadhi-khandana/13)]. Nothing whatsoever is done apart from Hari; substance, action, time, intrinsic nature, and the very *jīva* subsist only by his favor, and Viṣṇu is explicitly the giver of knowledge, liberation, and bliss [[Anuvy. 1.1.13-15](cite://madhva/anuvyakhyana/1.1.13-15)]. Thus *kartṛtva* on the side of the soul is real but *paratantra*, whereas *svātantrya* in the strict sense belongs only to Nārāyaṇa [[Anuvy. 2.3.66-69](cite://madhva/anuvyakhyana/2.3.66-69)][[VTV 3.30](cite://madhva/visnu-tattva-vinirnaya/3.30)].

The *jīva* is Brahman's *aṃśa*; plurality and dependent designations such as servitude are scripturally taught, so finite selfhood is real without becoming absolutely external to Brahman [[VĀB 2.3.43](cite://vijnanabhiksu/vijnanamrta-bhasya/2.3.43)]. *Māyā* is interpreted here as real *prakṛti*, not as sheer epistemic defect, and manifestation therefore retains a genuine causal order under Maheśvara [[VĀB 1.1.5](cite://vijnanabhiksu/vijnanamrta-bhasya/1.1.5)]. On that basis, *kartṛtva* is attributable to the finite *jīva* as a dependent *aṃśa* acting within *prakṛti*, not as an independent *svatantra* locus [[VĀB 2.3.43](cite://vijnanabhiksu/vijnanamrta-bhasya/2.3.43)][[VĀB 1.1.5](cite://vijnanabhiksu/vijnanamrta-bhasya/1.1.5)].

The *jīva* is *jñānasvarūpa*, atomic, distinct in each body, and *harer adhīna*; its agency is therefore real but irreducibly dependent [[DaŚ 1](cite://nimbarka/dasa-shloki/1)]. The school's *aṃśa* doctrine makes that dependence ontological rather than merely accidental: plurality is scripturally taught, and the living self is the eternal part of Bhagavān [[BS 2.3.42](cite://nimbarka/vedanta-parijata-saurabha/2.3.42)][[Gītā 15.7a](cite://nimbarka/vedanta-parijata-saurabha/15.7a)]. Bound, liberated, and devotional states belong to the same real *jīva*, so *kartṛtva* persists as the condition of a dependent self rather than as an autonomous substance [[DaŚ 2](cite://nimbarka/dasa-shloki/2)].

At the highest level, Brahman's own *kartṛ-bhāva* is explicit: it makes itself as itself, yet without forfeiting *avikṛtatva* [[BSB 1.4.26](cite://vallabha/anu-bhasya/1.4.26)][[BSB 1.4.26a](cite://vallabha/anubhasya/1.4.26a)]. This means that causality is a real *pariṇāma* of Brahman rather than a merely apparent projection, so agency is not expelled from the absolute but understood as its non-ordinary self-manifestation [[BSB 1.4.26](cite://vallabha/anu-bhasya/1.4.26)]. On the side of the finite self, the *jīva* is Brahman's *aṃśa* as taught by śruti-smṛti, and its finite agency is intelligible only within that real part-whole relation [[BSB 2.3.43-45](cite://vallabha/anu-bhasya/2.3.43-45)].

In Sāṅkhya, the conscious *puruṣa* is a witness and seer whose essential status is *sākṣitva*, *draṣṭṛtva*, and *akartṛbhāva*; the guṇas are the ones that function as agents [[SK 19-20](cite://isvarakrsna/sankhya-karika/19-20)]. The commentarial tradition makes the point explicit: *mahadādi-liṅga* performs cognitive and conative functions, while the puruṣa is called a doer only by conjunction, *kartṛsaṃsargāt karteva*, though in truth it is *akartā* [[SK Comm. 20](cite://isvarakrsna/sankhya-karika-comm/20)]. Accordingly bondage, transmigration, and release strictly belong to *prakṛti* and its evolutes, not to the puruṣa itself [[SK Comm. 62](cite://isvarakrsna/sankhya-karika-comm/62)].

In Yoga, the *dṛśya* made of guṇas is what acts, while the puruṣa is *draṣṭṛ*, pure seeing, to whom the products of buddhi are only secondarily attributed because it is the experiencer of their fruit [[YBh 2.18](cite://vyasa/yoga-bhasya/2.18)][[YS 2.20](cite://patanjali/yoga-sutra/2.20)]. Vyāsa states the point without ambiguity: the three guṇas are *kartṛs*, puruṣa is *akartṛ*, and bondage and release occur in buddhi though they are spoken of as belonging to puruṣa as *bhoktṛ* [[YBh 2.18](cite://vyasa/yoga-bhasya/2.18)]. Hence the language of action applied to puruṣa is only a *vyapadeśa* imposed on a *niṣkriya* subject [[YBh 1.9](cite://vyasa/yoga-bhasya/1.9)].

For Nyāya-Vaiśeṣika, the self is a real substance whose presence is inferred from desire, aversion, effort, pleasure, pain, and cognition; these are the marks of the self rather than of body alone [[NS 1.1.10](cite://gautama/nyaya-sutra/1.1.10)][[VS 3.2.4](cite://kanada/vaisesika-sutra/3.2.4)]. Agency is therefore anchored in the self through *prayatna*: bodily motion occurs from the conjunction of self and effort, and ethically charged activity proceeds from desire and aversion [[VS 5.1.1](cite://kanada/vaisesika-sutra/5.1.1)][[VS 6.2.17](cite://kanada/vaisesika-sutra/6.2.17)]. In later compendious idiom, *ātmā* is the substrate of cognition and *prayatna* is simply *kṛti*; *kartṛtva* thus belongs to an enduring self that initiates action through its qualities and connections [[AnTS 17](cite://annambhatta/tarka-sangraha/17)][[AnTS 70](cite://annambhatta/tarka-sangraha/70)].

In Pūrva-Mīmāṃsā, the relevant *kartṛ* is the ritual *puruṣa* or *yajamāna* to whom the फल belongs, while the sacrifice's limbs are ordered as *śeṣa* for the sake of that *puruṣārtha* [[MS 3.1.5-6](cite://jaimini/mimamsa-sutra/3.1.5-6)]. Śabara makes the agentive semantics explicit when glossing *dharma*: whoever is the doer of something is designated by that, so the act that yokes a person to *niḥśreyasa* is what receives the name *dharma* [[ŚBh on MS 1.1.2](cite://shabara/mimamsa-sutra-bhasya/1.1.2)]. At the level of ritual interpretation, *yaj* is parsed through its connection with dravya, phala, and bhoktṛ; agency is thus the authorized performance of a Vedic act that produces *apūrva* for the entitled performer [[MS 2.3.14](cite://jaimini/mimamsa-sutra/2.3.14)][[ŚBh on MS 7.4.1](cite://shabara/mimamsa-sutra-bhasya/7.4.1)].

The Madhyamaka critique refuses any ultimately established triad of *kartā*, *kriyā*, and *karaṇa*; to reify one is to reify the whole network of dependent designations [[MMK 24.17](cite://nagarjuna/mula-madhyamaka-karika/24.17)]. Since even self and not-self are only pedagogical designations, agenthood has no *svabhāva* that could underwrite an independent doer [[MMK 18.6](cite://nagarjuna/mula-madhyamaka-karika/18.6)]. Bondage is undone not by locating a metaphysical agent behind action but by tracing karma and kleśa back to *vikalpa* and *prapañca*, which cease in śūnyatā [[MMK 18.5](cite://nagarjuna/mula-madhyamaka-karika/18.5)].

What ordinary discourse calls self, agent, and object is an *upacāra* supervening on the transformation of consciousness; the manifold attribution of *ātman* and *dharma* unfolds only within *vijñāna-pariṇāma* [[Triṃśikā 1](cite://vasubandhu/trimsika/1)]. Because conceptual construction projects what it then treats as object, the agent-side of action is likewise a *vikalpa*-dependent presentation rather than an independently grounded self [[Triṃśikā 17](cite://vasubandhu/trimsika/17)]. Karmic perfumings, together with the perfuming of grasper-grasped duality, generate further maturation, so *kartṛtva* is locatable only within the conditioned stream and not in a persisting substantial subject [[Triṃśikā 19](cite://vasubandhu/trimsika/19)].

In Pratyabhijñā/Trika, *kartṛtva* in the strict sense is identical with Śiva's *svātantrya*: the five acts of manifestation, maintenance, withdrawal, concealment, and grace are themselves the mark of conscious lordship [[AbhT 14.24-27](cite://abhinavagupta/tantraloka/14.24-27)]. Because *asvatantrasya kartṛtvaṃ na hi jātūpapadyate*, potter-like efficient agency is secondary; primary agency is sovereign consciousness itself [[AbhT 9.8](cite://abhinavagupta/tantraloka/9.8)][[PH 34](cite://ksemaraja/pratyabhijna-hrdayam/34)]. Finite agency is contracted Śiva-agency: when *kriyāśakti* shrinks from *sarvakartṛtva* into *kiṃcitkartṛtva*, the saṃsārin emerges, and reawakening carries even the instruments back into *kartṛtā* [[PH 9-10](cite://ksemaraja/pratyabhijna-hrdayam/9-10)][[AbhT 30.2-3](cite://abhinavagupta/tantraloka/30.2-3)].

In Jainism, the soul is characterized by *upayoga*, and its karmically decisive activity is analyzed as *yoga*, the operations of body, speech, and mind [[TS 2.8](cite://umasvati/tattvartha-sutra/2.8)][[TS 6.1-2](cite://umasvati/tattvartha-sutra/6.1-2)]. These yogas are the channels of *āsrava*, and when accompanied by *kaṣāya* the jīva appropriates karmically fit pudgalas, which is precisely *bandha* [[TS 8.1-2](cite://umasvati/tattvartha-sutra/8.1-2)]. The Jain analogue of *kartṛtva*, then, is the soul's own passion-conditioned activity and appropriation, not an inactive witnessing consciousness [[TS 6.1-2](cite://umasvati/tattvartha-sutra/6.1-2)][[TS 8.1-2](cite://umasvati/tattvartha-sutra/8.1-2)].

translator_note: 'Agent' is the standard gloss and is acceptable, but the Pāṇinian definition (*svatantraḥ kartā*) couples *kartṛtva* tightly to *svātantrya* (independence) — a connection the English 'agent' obscures. The deeper translation problem: in Sāṅkhya and Advaita the conscious self (*puruṣa*, *ātman*) is *not* the *kartṛ*, while in Viśiṣṭādvaita, Tattva-vāda, and Trika the *kartṛ*-question reaches up to the divine. Translating *kartṛtva* as 'agency' often imports a modern free-will picture that the Indian schools are precisely arguing about; readers should be alerted that 'agency' here is a contested technical role, not a default psychological capacity.
